skill.name: azure-keyvault-py
skill.description: Guides Python developers through Azure Key Vault secrets, keys, certificates, cryptographic operations, async clients, and error handling using Azure SDK packages.
skill.summary: Build Python integrations for Azure Key Vault secrets, keys, certificates, and cryptographic operations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline code examples. The skill is legitimate Azure Key Vault documentation, but it includes examples that print secret values and retrieve certificate private key material, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Secret Values Printed to Output
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples retrieve secret values and print them to standard output. This is dangerous because logs, terminal history, or monitoring systems can capture the secret value.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly show retrieved secret values being printed. The context is instructional, not malicious, but the unsafe handling pattern is explicit.

medium_findings.title: Certificate Private Key Material Handling Example
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The certificate example retrieves certificate private key material through SecretClient. This is a legitimate Azure Key Vault pattern, but it needs stronger warnings because PEM or PKCS12 data must not be logged or stored insecurely.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly states that the secret value contains PEM or PKCS12 private key material. Legitimate administrative use is possible, so the concern is handling risk rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are documentation examples and inline package names, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited locations are inside Markdown code fences or inline table formatting. No Ruby code or autonomous command execution mechanism is present in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Azure Vault Placeholder URLs Are Expected Network Configuration
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are Azure Key Vault placeholder endpoints used to show required SDK configuration. They do not point to an attacker-controlled destination or include evidence of data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use placeholder vault names under the expected vault.azure.net domain. This matches normal Azure Key Vault SDK usage.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hit Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography at the metadata description, but no weak algorithm is specified there. The visible examples use RSA OAEP, SHA-256 digests, and RS256 signatures.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged description only names cryptographic key management. The concrete cryptographic examples use commonly accepted algorithms in the cited lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Printing Retrieved Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples print secret values after retrieval. Users should redact values and avoid sending secrets to logs or terminal output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The printed value is the direct result of get_secret. This is a clear unsafe example even though it appears instructional.

dangerous_patterns.title: Handling Private Certificate Material
dangerous_patterns.description: The certificate workflow retrieves PEM or PKCS12 material as a secret. This requires strict access control, memory handling, and logging controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe private key material in the secret value. The risk depends on how generated code uses the value.

content.user_title: Build Azure Key Vault Python Clients
content.value_statement: Python teams need reliable patterns for using Azure Key Vault without mixing up secrets, keys, and certificates. This skill provides focused SDK examples for clients, operations, crypto workflows, async usage, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows package installation commands for Azure Key Vault Python SDK clients.
Demonstrates SecretClient setup, secret creation, retrieval, listing, deletion, purge, and recovery.
Demonstrates KeyClient setup, RSA and elliptic curve key creation, retrieval, listing, and deletion.
Shows CryptographyClient encryption, decryption, signing, and verification examples.
Covers CertificateClient setup, certificate creation, retrieval, listing, deletion, and private key retrieval through secrets.
Includes async SecretClient usage and Azure SDK error handling patterns.
limitations: Does not provide a complete production application or deployment template.
Does not configure Azure RBAC, managed identities, or Key Vault resources for the user.
Some examples print secret values and require redaction before production use.
Does not cover automated secret rotation workflows in detail.

use_cases.title: Add Secret Retrieval to a Python Service
use_cases.description: Use the SecretClient setup and retrieval examples to connect an application to Azure Key Vault.

use_cases.title: Plan Key Vault Client Coverage
use_cases.description: Compare SecretClient, KeyClient, CryptographyClient, and CertificateClient responsibilities before designing an integration.

use_cases.title: Review Secure SDK Usage
use_cases.description: Use the best practices and error handling sections to review authentication, RBAC, soft delete, and exception handling.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Secret Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show how to install the required packages and create a Python SecretClient for my Azure Key Vault.

prompt_templates.title: Manage Secret Lifecycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline Python steps for setting, reading, listing versions, deleting, recovering, and purging a Key Vault secret.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Correct Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare SecretClient, KeyClient, CryptographyClient, and CertificateClient for a Python service design.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Key Vault Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Python Azure Key Vault integration for authentication, RBAC, async usage, exception handling, and unsafe secret output.

output_examples.input: Explain which Azure Key Vault Python client I need for storing database passwords.
output_examples.output: Use SecretClient from the secrets package. It stores and retrieves secret values, while KeyClient manages cryptographic keys and CertificateClient manages certificates.

output_examples.input: Summarize the safe setup pattern for a Python app using Key Vault.
output: Install the matching Azure SDK package and azure-identity.

output_examples.input: What should I check when access is denied?
output_examples.output: Check RBAC permissions, confirm the identity selected by DefaultAzureCredential, verify the vault URL, and handle Azure SDK HTTP errors explicitly.

best_practices: Use managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential instead of embedding credentials in code.
Redact secret values in generated examples, logs, terminal output, and test fixtures.
Prefer RBAC, soft delete, regular rotation, and version-aware retrieval for production systems.
anti_patterns: Do not print secret values or private key material while adapting the examples.
Do not hardcode a real vault URL, secret name, or secret value in shared source code.
Do not use purge operations in production workflows without explicit approval and recovery planning.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It provides Python SDK usage examples and does not provision Key Vaults, identities, or RBAC assignments.

faq.question: Which Azure authentication method does it show?
faq.answer: It uses DefaultAzureCredential, which can work with managed identity, developer login, and other Azure identity sources.

faq.question: Does it cover secrets, keys, and certificates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for SecretClient, KeyClient, CryptographyClient, and CertificateClient.

faq.question: Can I use the examples directly in production?
faq.answer: Treat them as reference patterns. Remove any secret printing, configure permissions, and add operational safeguards first.

faq.question: Does it include async Python examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an async SecretClient example for high-throughput scenarios.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it includes examples that print secret values and retrieve private certificate material.
